questions.question: In a ring buffer with head and tail indices, what's the problem that head==tail creates?
options: It's ambiguous between empty (just emptied) and full (just wrapped to meet head). Fix: separate len counter, sacrifice one slot, or uncapped indices.
questions.explanation: head and tail both wrap modulo cap. So head == tail can mean 0 elements (empty) or cap elements (full). Three standard fixes: (1) keep a separate `len`; (2) leave one slot unused, full = (tail+1 == head); (3) use uncapped size_t indices (`tail - head` gives len, with size_t wraparound after 2^64 ops). The lesson's main.c uses (3) — clean and branch-free.

questions.question: Why is power-of-2 capacity standard for ring buffers?
options: Modulo becomes bitwise AND: (i+1) & (cap-1). Single-cycle vs 20-30 cycles for integer divide. Critical when the queue is in a hot inner loop.
questions.explanation: Modern CPUs have integer divide at 20-40 cycles latency; bitwise AND is 1 cycle. For a ring buffer that does an indexing op per push and per pop, that's the difference between a 100M-msg/s queue and a 5M-msg/s queue. Linux, Rust's VecDeque, Java's ArrayDeque, every audio buffer — all power of 2.

questions.question: In a lock-free SPSC, why is the producer's tail store `Release` and the consumer's tail load `Acquire`?
options: Release-store ensures the data write happens BEFORE the tail update is visible. Acquire-load ensures any read AFTER the load sees the writes that happened before the matching release-store. Together: happens-before relationship → safe.
questions.explanation: On weakly-ordered CPUs (ARM, POWER), the compiler/CPU can reorder a write into the buffer past the tail update. If the consumer sees the new tail but loads the old buffer value, the queue silently corrupts. Release-store + Acquire-load create the happens-before edge that forbids this reordering. On x86 the loads/stores are stronger by default — but the code is portable only with explicit orderings.

questions.question: An overwriting trace buffer pushes when full by:
options: Dropping the oldest (advance head). Used for tracing/perf/postmortem buffers — losing old data is acceptable; producer must never wait.
questions.explanation: Tracing buffers (Linux perf, ftrace, ETW, packet capture) keep the most recent N samples regardless of how fast the producer is. Producer never blocks, never allocates, never fails — these would distort the very system being traced. The cost: old data is lost if the consumer is slow. For a transmit queue, you'd want the opposite policy (block or drop newest).

questions.question: What does the LMAX Disruptor achieve that a naive lock-free ring doesn't?
options: Multi-producer multi-consumer with batching: producers claim ranges of slots via a shared sequence, then write in parallel. Consumers batch process. Used in low-latency trading at >6M msg/s with deterministic latency.
questions.explanation: Naive SPSC handles only one producer + one consumer. Disruptor's innovations: (1) producers do compare_exchange on a shared sequence to claim slots, then write in parallel; (2) consumers track their own sequences; (3) batching amortizes the atomic costs; (4) padding eliminates false sharing between sequences. Achieves microsecond-scale latency at multi-million msg/s — the standard for HFT systems.

questions.question: Why does using `size_t` uncapped indices work even when the index overflows?
options: Unsigned overflow is well-defined to wrap modulo 2^N. `tail - head` is correct even after either wraps, because the SUBTRACTION also wraps modulo 2^N. As long as len < 2^N, you can recover the correct difference.
questions.explanation: Two's-complement and unsigned arithmetic both wrap. If tail = 2^64 - 5 and head = 2^64 - 10, then tail - head = 5 — correct. The CPU's arithmetic does this naturally. The trick works for any cap up to 2^63 (avoiding signed overflow ambiguities in C). At a steady 1 GHz of operations, the indices wrap every ~580 years — effectively never.
